# Frame & Light — Episode
## Lighting Decisions That Save a Shoot
Host: Leo
Guest: Daniel Olufemi
#fixture-v3: failure_modes=frame_topic_cross_domain,same_first_distinct
#fixture-v3: voice=en-NG host_voice=en-GB

[00:00]
Leo: Welcome back to Frame & Light. Today we're talking about lighting design, and I'm joined by Daniel Olufemi, commercial lighting director. Daniel Olufemi, thanks for being here.
Daniel Olufemi: Thanks, Leo. Great to be back.

Leo: This episode is brought to you by Adobe. Adobe Creative Cloud puts Lightroom, Photoshop, and Premiere on every device. Get started at adobe.com/podcast.

[03:30]
Leo: Walk me through a concrete example. What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Daniel Olufemi: Here's how I think about it. On location, the first decision is always: am I shaping the existing light? We've seen this play out at teams using Adobe. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Leo: That's a useful framing. How does Profoto fit into this picture?
Daniel Olufemi: Yeah, so — Two-light setups solve 80% of commercial problems. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Leo: Walk me through a concrete example. What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Daniel Olufemi: Honestly, Frame and light are inseparable. We've seen this play out at teams using Capture One. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Leo: Where does that break down?
Daniel Olufemi: Outside of Capture One-class tooling, the failure mode is harder to spot. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Leo: What's the one heuristic you keep coming back to? How does Profoto fit into this picture?
Daniel Olufemi: Let me give you the version I actually believe. Color temperature mistakes cost more shoots than focus mistakes. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.

[12:00]
Leo: We'll be right back after a quick word from our sponsors.
Ad: Today's episode is sponsored by Peak Design. Peak Design builds camera bags, straps, and tripods designed by photographers. Visit peakdesign.com/podcast for a free trial.
Leo: Welcome back to the show.

[14:00]
Leo: Push back on the conventional wisdom for me. How does Peak Design fit into this picture?
Daniel Olufemi: Honestly, On location, the first decision is always: am I shaping the existing light? We've seen this play out at teams using Adobe. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're making.
Leo: Push back on the conventional wisdom for me. What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Daniel Olufemi: Let me give you the version I actually believe. Two-light setups solve 80% of commercial problems. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Leo: Walk me through a concrete example. Where do strobe lighting and lighting design intersect?
Daniel Olufemi: Yeah, so — Frame and light are inseparable. We've seen this play out at teams using Peak Design. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're making.
Leo: That maps to what I've seen.
Daniel Olufemi: Outside of Profoto-class tooling, the failure mode is harder to spot. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're making.
Leo: What's the one heuristic you keep coming back to? How does Adobe fit into this picture?
Daniel Olufemi: It comes down to this: Color temperature mistakes cost more shoots than focus mistakes. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.

[22:00]
Leo: Let's pivot — frame is where I want to spend the last stretch.
Daniel Olufemi: That's where the conversation gets interesting, because the trade-offs around frame cross-cut everything we've covered.
Leo: Take me through the decision. Where do strobe lighting and lighting design intersect?
Daniel Olufemi: Yeah, so — On location, the first decision is always: am I shaping the existing light? We've seen this play out at teams using Capture One. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Leo: What's the one heuristic you keep coming back to? What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Daniel Olufemi: Honestly, Two-light setups solve 80% of commercial problems. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Leo: That's a useful framing. What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Daniel Olufemi: Sure. Frame and light are inseparable. We've seen this play out at teams using Peak Design. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're making.
Leo: Say more about that.
Daniel Olufemi: And when teams adopt Adobe in that flow, the trade-off becomes visible — The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're making.
Leo: That's a useful framing. What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Daniel Olufemi: Let me give you the version I actually believe. Color temperature mistakes cost more shoots than focus mistakes. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.

[28:00]
Leo: Before we wrap, what's one thing listeners can try this week?
Daniel Olufemi: Pick one of the ideas we covered and run it for two weeks. The rest is iteration.

Leo: Before we wrap, thanks again to our friends at Stripe. Stripe makes payments simple, whether you're running a marketplace or a subscription business.

Leo: Daniel Olufemi, thanks for the conversation.
Daniel Olufemi: Thanks, Leo.
Leo: That's it for today's episode of Frame & Light. See you next time.
